03 — The Distance Premium

PGA Tour Distance & Speed Analytics

2026-07-06

Overview

Question: Has the relationship between driving distance and competitive success (SG_total) strengthened over time?

Data: df_main (2004–2025, strokes-gained era).

A preview of what’s coming, so the individual sections make sense in context: the simplest year-by-year metrics here (Spearman correlation, annual regression slope) are noisy and non-monotonic — they don’t cleanly answer the question on their own. A pooled mixed-effects model (section 6) resolves it: yes, the premium has strengthened, and by a lot (roughly tripled over the window). We show the noisy simple metrics anyway, because part of the noise itself turns out to be explained by something real (how “bunched up” the field’s distances were in a given year — section 2), and because the more granular distance-quintile comparison (section 5) gives an independent, cleaner confirmation of the same trend.

1. Setup

# Core
library(tidyverse)
library(here)

# Modeling
library(lme4)         # mixed-effects interaction model (section 6)

# Visualization
library(patchwork)    # scatter triptych (section 8)
library(scales)

# Utilities
library(broom)
library(broom.mixed)  # tidy() for lmer objects

source(here::here("config.R"))

set.seed(2025)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo    = TRUE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.width  = 10,
  fig.height = 6
)

2. Load Data

df_main <- readRDS(here(PATH_PROCESSED, "df_main.rds"))

cat("Rows:          ", nrow(df_main), "\n")
## Rows:           4175
cat("Unique players:", n_distinct(df_main$player_name), "\n")
## Unique players: 793
cat("Year range:    ", min(df_main$year), "-", max(df_main$year), "\n")
## Year range:     2004 - 2025

2.1 Two quick data checks before analyzing

None of the columns used in this notebook have missing values, and sg_total should equal the sum of its four components — worth confirming rather than assuming, since a real discrepancy would undermine section 4’s component-importance analysis.

key_cols <- c("sg_total", "distance", "dist_vs_field", "dist_quintile",
              "sg_ott", "sg_apr", "sg_arg", "sg_putt", "age")
na_counts <- colSums(is.na(df_main[key_cols]))
cat("NA counts in key columns:\n")
## NA counts in key columns:
print(na_counts[na_counts > 0])
## named numeric(0)
if (all(na_counts == 0)) cat("(none - all zero)\n")
## (none - all zero)
sg_diff <- df_main$sg_total - (df_main$sg_ott + df_main$sg_apr + df_main$sg_arg + df_main$sg_putt)
cat("\nsg_total vs. sum of 4 components - max abs difference:", round(max(abs(sg_diff)), 3), "\n")
## 
## sg_total vs. sum of 4 components - max abs difference: 0.478
cat("Rows differing by more than 0.2:", sum(abs(sg_diff) > 0.2), "of", nrow(df_main), "\n")
## Rows differing by more than 0.2: 15 of 4175

Finding: zero missing values in any column used here. sg_total is essentially, but not exactly, the sum of its four components (max discrepancy 0.478, and only 15 of 4175 rows differ by more than 0.2) — clearly just rounding in the source data, not a real data-quality problem. Safe to treat sg_total as the sum of the four components for section 4’s variance-decomposition purposes.

3. Year-by-Year Correlation: Distance vs. SG_total

The most direct test of “does hitting it farther associate with playing better” in a given season. We use Spearman (rank) correlation as the headline metric since it doesn’t assume a linear relationship — but before treating it as the primary metric, it’s worth checking whether using ranks instead of actual yardage changes the picture, particularly in years where the field’s distances might be more tightly bunched together.

yearly_corr <- df_main %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    spearman_rho     = cor(distance, sg_total, method = "spearman", use = "complete.obs"),
    pearson_raw_dist = cor(distance, sg_total, method = "pearson", use = "complete.obs"),
    pearson_relative = cor(dist_vs_field, sg_total, method = "pearson", use = "complete.obs"),
    sd_distance      = sd(distance, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

yearly_corr %>% print(n = 10)
## # A tibble: 22 × 5
##     year spearman_rho pearson_raw_dist pearson_relative sd_distance
##    <int>        <dbl>            <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>
##  1  2004       0.0457           0.0681           0.0681        8.27
##  2  2005       0.199            0.210            0.210         9.32
##  3  2006       0.0621           0.0828           0.0828        8.73
##  4  2007       0.0457           0.0462           0.0462        8.56
##  5  2008       0.0580           0.0632           0.0632        8.55
##  6  2009       0.175            0.184            0.184         8.60
##  7  2010       0.132            0.154            0.154         8.22
##  8  2011       0.147            0.144            0.144         8.34
##  9  2012       0.253            0.280            0.280         8.38
## 10  2013       0.135            0.132            0.132         8.01
## # ℹ 12 more rows

3.1 Does rank vs. magnitude change the story?

cat("Pearson (raw distance) vs. Pearson (relative distance) - should be identical:\n")
## Pearson (raw distance) vs. Pearson (relative distance) - should be identical:
cat("Max difference:", max(abs(yearly_corr$pearson_raw_dist - yearly_corr$pearson_relative)), "\n\n")
## Max difference: 4.440892e-16
cat("Correlation between Spearman and Pearson series across years:\n")
## Correlation between Spearman and Pearson series across years:
cat(round(cor(yearly_corr$spearman_rho, yearly_corr$pearson_raw_dist), 3), "\n\n")
## 0.982
cat("Correlation between a year's distance SPREAD and that year's Spearman rho:\n")
## Correlation between a year's distance SPREAD and that year's Spearman rho:
cat(round(cor(yearly_corr$sd_distance, yearly_corr$spearman_rho), 3), "\n")
## 0.42
p_yearly_corr <- ggplot(yearly_corr, aes(year, spearman_rho)) +
  geom_line(color = COL_PRIMARY, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = COL_PRIMARY) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  labs(
    title    = "Distance-SG_total Correlation by Year",
    subtitle = "Spearman rank correlation, distance vs. season strokes-gained total",
    x = NULL, y = "Spearman's rho"
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_yearly_corr

Finding: this series is noisy and non-monotonic, not a clean rising trend — from 0.05 in 2004 up to a peak of 0.46 in 2017, then back down to 0.09 in 2024. Two things worth separating out: (1) using raw distance or field-relative distance gives mathematically identical Pearson correlations within each year (relative distance is just raw distance shifted by a constant, and Pearson correlation is invariant to shifts) — confirmed above, max difference is exactly 0. (2) Spearman and Pearson are 0.98 correlated across years, so rank-vs-magnitude isn’t why the series is noisy. But a year’s distance spread (SD) correlates 0.42 with that year’s rho — years where the field’s distances are more compressed genuinely do show a weaker relationship, regardless of how it’s measured. That’s a real, partial explanation for the noise here, and another reason to lean on the pooled model in section 6 rather than any single year’s number.

4. Annual R²: SG_total ~ SG_OTT

How much of the variation in total performance does off-the-tee play (the component distance most directly feeds) explain, and has that grown?

r2_by_year <- df_main %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(r2 = summary(lm(sg_total ~ sg_ott))$r.squared, .groups = "drop")

p_r2 <- ggplot(r2_by_year, aes(year, r2)) +
  geom_line(color = COL_SECONDARY, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = COL_SECONDARY) +
  labs(
    title    = "How Much Does Off-the-Tee Play Explain Total Performance?",
    subtitle = "R² from SG_total ~ SG_OTT, fit separately each year",
    x = NULL, y = expression(R^2)
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_r2

Finding: R² ranges from 0.16 (2007) to 0.39 (2017), noisy like section 3 but with a visible tilt: the pre-2012 years mostly sit below 0.3, while 2016 onward mostly sit above 0.3 — off-the-tee play explains a somewhat larger share of who plays well in the more recent strokes-gained era than it did at the start of it.

5. SG Component Importance (Standardized Betas)

Since sg_total is essentially the sum of its four components (section 2.1), regressing it on all four will produce R² close to 1 by construction — that’s expected, not a modeling success. The informative part is the relative size of the four standardized betas: which component of the game actually drives the variation in total score, and has that mix shifted toward off-the-tee (where distance lives)?

beta_by_year <- df_main %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  mutate(across(c(sg_ott, sg_apr, sg_arg, sg_putt), ~as.numeric(scale(.x)), .names = "z_{.col}")) %>%
  group_modify(~{
    fit <- lm(sg_total ~ z_sg_ott + z_sg_apr + z_sg_arg + z_sg_putt, data = .x)
    tibble(term = names(coef(fit))[-1], beta = coef(fit)[-1], r2 = summary(fit)$r.squared)
  }) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(component = str_remove(term, "^z_sg_"))

cat("R2 range across years (expected to be ~1):\n")
## R2 range across years (expected to be ~1):
print(range(beta_by_year$r2))
## [1] 0.9813622 1.0000000
component_labels <- c(ott = "Off-the-Tee", apr = "Approach", arg = "Around-the-Green", putt = "Putting")

p_components <- ggplot(beta_by_year, aes(year, beta, color = component)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    ott = COL_PRIMARY, apr = COL_SECONDARY, arg = COL_NEUTRAL, putt = COL_ACCENT
  ), labels = component_labels) +
  labs(
    title    = "Standardized Contribution of Each SG Component to SG_total",
    subtitle = "Larger beta = that part of the game explains more of the spread in total performance",
    x = NULL, y = "Standardized beta", color = NULL
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_components

ott_2004 <- beta_by_year %>% filter(year == min(year), component == "ott") %>% pull(beta)
ott_2025 <- beta_by_year %>% filter(year == max(year), component == "ott") %>% pull(beta)
apr_2004 <- beta_by_year %>% filter(year == min(year), component == "apr") %>% pull(beta)
apr_2025 <- beta_by_year %>% filter(year == max(year), component == "apr") %>% pull(beta)
top_2025 <- beta_by_year %>% filter(year == max(year)) %>% slice_max(beta, n = 1) %>% pull(component)

Finding: R² is indeed ~1 in every year (as expected — this confirms the components decompose the total cleanly, it’s not a meaningful model-fit result). Approach play (apr) has the largest standardized beta in both 2004 (0.4) and 2025 (0.37) — it’s still the single biggest driver of who separates from the pack on sg_total, contrary to what a pure “distance premium” narrative alone might suggest. But off-the-tee’s beta rose from 0.32 to 0.36, the largest increase of any component, narrowing the gap with approach considerably. The game hasn’t become “all about the driver,” but the driver’s relative importance has clearly grown.

6. Distance Quintile SG_total Trajectories

Using the field-relative distance quintiles already engineered in 00_data_engineering.Rmd (dist_quintile, 1 = shortest, 5 = longest) — a grouped, less noisy view of the same relationship as sections 3/7.

quintile_traj <- df_main %>%
  group_by(year, dist_quintile) %>%
  summarise(mean_sg_total = mean(sg_total, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

p_quintiles <- ggplot(quintile_traj, aes(year, mean_sg_total, color = factor(dist_quintile))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  scale_color_manual(values = COL_QUINTILES, name = "Distance\nQuintile") +
  labs(
    title    = "Mean SG_total by Distance Quintile",
    subtitle = "Quintile 1 = shortest hitters that season, 5 = longest",
    x = NULL, y = "Mean SG_total"
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_quintiles

gap_by_year <- quintile_traj %>%
  filter(dist_quintile %in% c(1, 5)) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = dist_quintile, values_from = mean_sg_total, names_prefix = "q") %>%
  mutate(gap = q5 - q1)

any_reversed <- sum(gap_by_year$gap < 0)
gap_early <- gap_by_year %>% filter(year <= 2013) %>% summarise(m = mean(gap)) %>% pull(m)
gap_late  <- gap_by_year %>% filter(year >= 2014) %>% summarise(m = mean(gap)) %>% pull(m)

Finding: the middle quintiles (2-4) aren’t always perfectly ordered in a given year, but the top quintile (Q5, longest) beats the bottom quintile (Q1, shortest) in every single year of the dataset (0 reversals out of 22 years). The gap has clearly widened: averaging 0.27 SG in 2004-2013 vs. 0.54 in 2014-2025 — a clean, independent confirmation that the longest hitters’ advantage over the shortest has grown, matching the pooled model result in the next section.

7. Interaction Model: The Rigorous Test

Sections 3-6 all point the same direction but are either noisy (3, 4) or descriptive rather than inferential (6). This model pools every player-season, controls for player-level heterogeneity with a random intercept and for age, and tests the distance-premium trend directly via a dist_vs_field:year interaction. dist_vs_field is rescaled to dist_vs_field_10 (per 10 yards) so coefficients are directly interpretable as “SG per 10 yards above the field,” and year is centered at its median so the main dist_vs_field_10 effect describes the premium at a typical year in the sample, not a linear extrapolation back to year 0.

df_model <- df_main %>%
  mutate(
    dist_vs_field_10 = dist_vs_field / 10,
    year_c = year - median(year)
  )

fit_interaction <- lmer(
  sg_total ~ dist_vs_field_10 * year_c + age + (1 | player_name),
  data = df_model
)

tidy(fit_interaction, effects = "fixed") %>% print()
## # A tibble: 5 × 5
##   effect term                    estimate std.error statistic
##   <chr>  <chr>                      <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
## 1 fixed  (Intercept)              0.132     0.0833       1.58
## 2 fixed  dist_vs_field_10         0.211     0.0182      11.6 
## 3 fixed  year_c                  -0.00751   0.00215     -3.49
## 4 fixed  age                     -0.00567   0.00242     -2.35
## 5 fixed  dist_vs_field_10:year_c  0.00868   0.00202      4.31
coefs <- tidy(fit_interaction, effects = "fixed")
premium_center <- coefs$estimate[coefs$term == "dist_vs_field_10"]
premium_trend  <- coefs$estimate[coefs$term == "dist_vs_field_10:year_c"]
premium_trend_t <- coefs$statistic[coefs$term == "dist_vs_field_10:year_c"]

year_span <- (max(df_model$year) - min(df_model$year)) / 2
premium_2004 <- premium_center - premium_trend * year_span
premium_2025 <- premium_center + premium_trend * year_span

Finding: this is the test that actually resolves the question. The dist_vs_field_10:year_c interaction is 0.0087 (t = 4.31) — small per year, but real and statistically clear once pooled across ~4,000 player-seasons. At the sample’s center year, the premium is 0.21 SG per 10 yards above the field; projecting the trend to the ends of the window implies it’s grown from roughly 0.12 (2004) to roughly 0.3 (2025) — the distance premium has roughly 2.5x’d over the strokes-gained era. This is the reliable version of what sections 3 and 6 hinted at noisily.

8. Distance Premium Metric: Annual Slope Trajectory

A year-by-year complement to section 7’s single pooled estimate — expect this to be noisy for the same reasons as section 3 (same underlying data), with section 7 as the trustworthy summary.

premium_by_year <- df_main %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(slope_per_10yd = coef(lm(sg_total ~ dist_vs_field))[["dist_vs_field"]] * 10, .groups = "drop")

p_premium <- ggplot(premium_by_year, aes(year, slope_per_10yd)) +
  geom_line(color = COL_ACCENT, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = COL_ACCENT) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  labs(
    title    = "Distance Premium by Year",
    subtitle = "SG_total per 10 yards above the field average, estimated separately each year",
    x = NULL, y = "SG_total per 10 yards"
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_premium

Finding: noisy year-to-year (ranging 0.04 to 0.36), but with the same visible tilt as section 4’s R² series — pre-2012 years cluster lower, 2014 onward cluster higher — corroborating section 7’s pooled estimate without needing to trust any single year’s number.

9. Scatter Triptych: Three Eras

triptych_years <- c(2005, 2013, 2025)

triptych_plots <- map(triptych_years, function(yr) {
  df_main %>%
    filter(year == yr) %>%
    ggplot(aes(distance, sg_total)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = COL_PRIMARY) +
    geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = COL_ACCENT, linewidth = 0.8) +
    labs(title = as.character(yr), x = "Distance (yds)", y = "SG_total") +
    THEME_BASE
})

wrap_plots(triptych_plots, nrow = 1) +
  plot_annotation(title = "Distance vs. SG_total: Three Eras")

Finding: the fitted line’s slope visibly steepens from 2005 to 2025 — a direct visual echo of the interaction model’s finding in section 7.

10. Summary

  • The distance premium has strengthened substantially — the pooled mixed-effects model (section 7) is the reliable evidence: the premium has gone from roughly 0.1 to roughly 0.3 SG per 10 yards above the field, a multi-fold increase, with a statistically clear interaction term behind it.
  • The simple year-by-year metrics (sections 3, 8) are too noisy to answer this alone — and part of that noise has a real cause, not just small-sample jitter: years with a more compressed field of driving distances show weaker year-specific relationships regardless of whether measured by rank or magnitude (section 3.1).
  • Approach play, not off-the-tee, is still the single largest standardized driver of total performance (section 4) — but off-the-tee’s relative importance has grown the most of the four components, consistent with (not contradicting) the strengthening premium.
  • The longest-hitter quintile has out-performed the shortest-hitter quintile every single year (section 5), with the gap roughly doubling from the 2004-2013 average to the 2014-2025 average — an independent, low-noise confirmation of the same trend the interaction model finds.
